system_prompt:
You are distilling one chapter of a guitar method book into a short summary.

The user will give you a chapter file with topic-organized verbatim quotes
plus the human-rationale notes for each. Write a one-paragraph summary (4-7
sentences) that captures:

  - what this chapter teaches (the techniques, concepts, rules, examples)
  - the master's characteristic moves visible in this chapter (if any)
  - the load-bearing terminology this chapter introduces

Reference the topic labels from the chapter file when useful. Do not invent
information not present in the chapter. If the chapter is biographical or
introductory rather than instructional, say so explicitly.

Output just the paragraph — no preamble, no JSON, no markdown formatting.
user_prompt:
Chapter 1: How to Interpret the Diagrams

<<<CHAPTER FILE
# Chapter 1 — How to Interpret the Diagrams

_Pages 5-9._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## fingerstyle vs. flat pick

> the progressions in this book were geared to be played without a flat pick, that is, they are
> meant to be played using only the thumb and fingers of the right hand (however, if you are accustomed to using a
> thumb pick or finger picks or both, this book is also right up your alley).

**Significance (p. 6).** This establishes the method's foundational right-hand technique choice, which governs every exercise and chord voicing in the book.

## right-hand finger assignment

> Your thumb (T) should strike the lowest (thickest) string involved in the chord; your other fingers just get
> added on “‘in order” (starting with the Ist finger) according to how many other notes are in the chord.

**Significance (p. 6).** This is the core rule for right-hand fingering that students must apply to every chord in the method.

## right wrist position

> Holding your right wrist up away from the guitar puts your fingers into a position where it 1s easier to achieve
> good tone, accuracy and speed. This posture may seem 2 little awkward at first (mainly because it’s new to you) but
> gradually this becomes an incredibly relaxed way to play. As far as how high to hold the wrist, just start out with a
> moderate height {about 2 to 3 inches) and gradually you will find yourself going up higher automatically

**Significance (p. 6).** Wrist height is the key right-hand posture rule that determines tone quality and technical efficiency throughout the method.

## plucking technique / squeezing

> As far as the actual plucking of the strings goes, all you need to do is “squeeze” gently to produce a sound.
> Exceptions to this arise when dealing with a chord that has six notes init. In such a case, one recommended approach
> is to just use your thumb to strum all the strings.

**Significance (p. 6).** This defines the default right-hand attack mechanic and its one exception, giving beginners a complete plucking rule set.

## nail length for fingerstyle

> Almost all guitarists who use their bare thumb and fingers for plucking have found it necessary to let their nails
> grow here so as to produce a clearer and cleaner sound.

**Significance (p. 6).** Nail length is a physical prerequisite for achieving the tone quality the method demands from fingerstyle technique.

## double stop technique defined

> we will call the double stop. We can define the double stop as a technique where you come
> down with the tip of your finger right between two strings, so as to sound them both.

**Significance (p. 7).** This is the book's formal definition of double stop, a named left-hand technique that recurs throughout the method's chord voicings.

## double stop vs. flattening rule

> A general guideline for deciding when to flatten and when to double
> 4 2 TT] ¢ stop might be something like: If the two adjacent notes are at the
> top of the chord (in terms of pitch), use the flattening technique; if

**Significance (p. 7).** This rule tells students which of two distinct left-hand techniques to apply based on register, directly affecting playability of chord voicings.

## double stop notation convention

> double stop fingerings will
>
> Dev wal : f actually be notated with a little connecting line to distinguish them
> decal from the flattening technique

**Significance (p. 7).** Knowing this notation convention is prerequisite to correctly reading every diagram that uses adjacent-string fingerings.

## double stop benefits

> some of the most important benefits of the double stop technique
> are: 1) the ability to play chords that could not be played any other way, 2) the ability to play some chords on higher
> frets than would normally be practical, and 3) the freeing of an extra finger, which can be used for certain melodic
> effects while you sustain the chord

**Significance (p. 8).** Stating the three concrete benefits justifies why the method insists on this difficult technique and frames its strategic importance.

## cleanliness rule / arpeggiation check

> [t is very important to always strive for cleanliness in all chords; by cleanliness, it is meant that all notes which
> are supposed to be sounding should be doing just that.

**Significance (p. 8).** This is an explicit practice rule that defines the quality standard every chord must meet throughout the method.

## arpeggiation as accuracy check

> If your ear cannot detect these errors yet, try arpeggiating (holding the chord down and
>
> plucking each note of the chord one at a time) chords and progressions to check your accuracy.

**Significance (p. 8).** This diagnostic technique gives students a concrete practice tool for self-correcting chord errors before the ear is trained.

## 5th finger principle (Van Eps)

> If you encounter a diagram with indications for one finger on two different frets, don’t be alarmed; this is what
> George Van Eps calls the Sth finger principle, and you can be sure that it is practical.

**Significance (p. 9).** This names and explains the 5th finger principle, a specialized left-hand technique attributed to Van Eps that enables otherwise-impossible chord voicings.

## sustained note / melodic addition symbol

> In order to indicate moving melodic lines with a sustained chord, the following symbols are used: The symbol
> % indicates 4 note that is added to the chord, after the chord has been sounded, and while the chord is still ringing (iry
> not to muffle any of the ringing chord notes while you bring in this new note).

**Significance (p. 9).** This defines the notation system for melody-over-chord technique, which is central to the method's characteristic harmonic-melodic approach.

## left-hand finger numbering

> concerning left hand fingering: The index finger is considered to be the first finger, the middle finger
> is the second, and so on; if the thumb were to be used, it would be indicated by a T.

**Significance (p. 7).** This establishes the standard finger-numbering convention that must be understood to read every fingering diagram in the book.

## sustain tie symbol between diagrams

> This symbol between two notes of two different diagrams means that you should hold the note and
> let it ring but not pluck it again in the second diagram (in other words, let the note SUSTAIN from one chord to the
> next

**Significance (p. 7).** This notation rule governs how sustained common tones are executed across chord changes, a technique critical to smooth voice-leading in the progressions.

CHAPTER FILE>>>
